text: Spatial transcriptomics (ST) has transformed our ability to understand the heterogeneous organization of tissues by providing gene expression data with spatial context. To fully utilize this resource, computational methods are required that can segment tissue into its constituent domains, integrate data from multiple samples, and upscale low-resolution data from individual ST spots to single cells. Here, we introduce GraphST, a graph-based self-supervised contrastive learning framework that facilitates the comprehensive analysis of ST data. GraphST utilizes graph neural networks (GNNS) to learn the gene expression and spatial neighborhood information for each ST spot and then uses contrastive learning to enforce the latent representation to be sensitive to the spatial neighborhood information. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in spatial clustering on ST data from various platforms, including 10x Visium, Stereo-seq and Slide-seqV2, and that it outperforms other existing segmentation approaches in identifying detailed anatomical structures in brain and embryo tissues of developing embryos. Additionally, our GraphST method is capable of multi-sample integration and batch effect correction for aligning serial sections or adjacent tissue sections. For cell-type deconvolution, GraphST utilizes an augmentation-free contrastive learning strategy to align scRNA-seq data with ST data, which facilitates accurate mapping of cell types and infiltrating immune cells in heterogeneous environments such as breast cancer tumors and lymph nodes. GraphST combines the three analyses into a fast and versatile computational pipeline for the comprehensive analysis of multicellular tissues.
